---
title: "The same walk at two scales"
description: "One random-walk primitive suggests which wiki pages to read and orders which corpus chunks to extract: identical math, different graph."
date: "2026-07-20"
categories: [retrieval]
draft: false
---

::: {.callout-note title="Status"}
**Design.** The corpus side is demonstrated; the wiki side is unbuilt, with several open design questions.
:::

**Thesis.** One random-walk-with-restart primitive runs at two scales. The math is identical; only the graph and the seeding change.

At the **wiki** scale, the nodes are pages plus lightweight content nodes, and the edges are structural (frontmatter edges, wikilinks materialized as weighted `mentions`, inverses) and content-derived. Seed the walk from the concepts in a question, let it settle, and the top pages become a reading list, surfacing pages that name-matching would miss. At the **corpus** scale, the nodes are chunks, entities, and documents, and the edges are containment, chunk-mentions-entity, citation, and cooccurrence. Seed from the question's entities and the top chunks come back.

Why the same code serves both: a random walk with restart is a fixed-point iteration on a stochastic matrix. It cares about dimensionality, connectivity, the seed distribution, and the restart probability. It does not care whether a node is a page or a chunk. So one implementation, a graph loader, a seed extractor, a walk runner, a ranker, and a presenter, covers both layers, and on the wiki side the whole thing fits in a few hundred lines of Python.

The two layers compose in either order. Wiki first, then corpus: recall from durable memory, and drop down to the raw corpus for evidence the wiki has not yet cited. Corpus first, then wiki assembly: pull the chunks, then synthesize a new page and file it back into memory.

What is honestly unsettled: the wiki side has not been built. The restart probability needs tuning, the edge-weight schema is unfixed (the experiments have not been run), and a single joint wiki-plus-corpus graph is structurally attractive but empirically unvalidated. The claim here is that the primitive is the same, not that both halves have been shipped.
